techniques.name: Boolean-based blind
techniques.summary: Inject a condition; observe whether the response differs (true vs false). Slow but works without visible output.

techniques.name: Error-based
techniques.summary: Coerce the DB into a verbose error message containing the data being extracted. Fast when the app leaks errors.

techniques.name: UNION query
techniques.summary: Append a UNION SELECT to the original query to surface arbitrary data inline. Needs same column count and compatible types.

techniques.name: Stacked queries
techniques.summary: Send a second statement separated by `;`. Required for write operations on backends / drivers that allow multi-statement execution.

techniques.name: Time-based blind
techniques.summary: Use SLEEP() / WAITFOR DELAY to leak data via timing. Works when there's no visible output and no errors.

techniques.name: Inline queries
techniques.summary: Embed a sub-query in the original — niche, used when other techniques are blocked.

commonFlags.description: Target URL with at least one parameter to test.
Read a raw HTTP request from FILE. Best way to test authenticated / CSRF-bound endpoints — export from Burp.
POST body. Forces a POST request with this body.
Cookie header. For session-bound endpoints.
Additional headers. Repeatable. Use `--auth-type` for HTTP auth.
Force a specific HTTP method (PUT, DELETE, etc.).
Never prompt — accept defaults for every question. Required for unattended runs.
Detection breadth: how many parameters / locations are tested. Higher = more requests.
Detection depth: how aggressive payloads get. 3 enables OR-based and stacked-query payloads.
Skip detection — tell sqlmap the backend (mysql, postgresql, mssql, oracle, sqlite, ...).
Restrict detection to specific techniques (default = all). e.g. --technique=BT for blind only.
Apply tamper script(s) to bypass naive WAFs (space2comment, between, randomcase, ...).
Send all traffic through a proxy — usually `http://127.0.0.1:8080` to inspect in Burp.
Concurrent requests. Default 1; raise to 5-10 if the target tolerates it.
Randomise the User-Agent per request.
Show the connected DB user and current database.
Check whether the current DB user has admin privileges.
Enumerate databases, tables, columns. Combine with -D / -T / -C to scope.
Dump rows. --dump-all dumps every table sqlmap can reach (very loud).
Read a file from the DB host's filesystem (where supported).
Upload local file L to remote path R on the DB host.
Drop into an interactive OS shell on the DB host (DBA + writable webroot usually required).
Stage a Meterpreter / VNC payload on the DB host.

enumerationFlow.purpose: Quick fingerprint after sqlmap confirms injection — who are we, where are we, can we DBA?
Enumerate every accessible database on the server.
Pick a target database and list its tables.
See the column layout before dumping — avoid pulling 50 GB of useless blobs.
Targeted dump of just the interesting fields.
Pull the full schema of every accessible DB — a structural map for the report.

tamperScripts.name: space2comment
tamperScripts.purpose: Replace spaces with `/**/` — bypasses filters that strip spaces.

tamperScripts.name: between
tamperScripts.purpose: Replace `>` with `NOT BETWEEN 0 AND` to dodge `>`/`<` filters.

tamperScripts.name: randomcase
tamperScripts.purpose: Randomise the case of SQL keywords — defeats case-sensitive blacklists.

tamperScripts.name: charencode
tamperScripts.purpose: URL-encode every character in the payload — slips past simple keyword filters.

tamperScripts.name: modsecurityversioned
tamperScripts.purpose: Wrap payload in MySQL versioned comments to evade ModSecurity rules.

tamperScripts.name: 0eunion
tamperScripts.purpose: Replace `UNION SELECT` with `0eUNION SELECT` to evade naive blacklists.

recipes.title: Basic GET parameter test
recipes.details: The smallest viable run. sqlmap auto-detects the DBMS and tries every technique at default level/risk. --batch accepts all prompts.

recipes.title: POST data injection
recipes.details: Test a POST body. By default sqlmap tries every parameter; pin one with `-p user`.

recipes.title: From a Burp request file
recipes.details: Best for authenticated / CSRF-bound endpoints. Export the request from Burp (Action → Copy to file). Cookies, headers, body — all carried.

recipes.title: Dump a specific table
recipes.details: Target the dump precisely. sqlmap rate-limits dumps automatically and writes CSVs to ~/.local/share/sqlmap/output/.

recipes.title: OS shell on a vulnerable backend
recipes.details: Requires DBA privileges and (on most stacks) a writable webroot. sqlmap stages a small file uploader and gives you an interactive shell.

recipes.title: Pivot through Burp for inspection
recipes.details: Routes every sqlmap request through Burp so you can see exactly what payloads it's sending — invaluable for debugging false negatives.

recipes.title: WAF evasion with tamper scripts
recipes.details: Stack tamper scripts left-to-right. --delay slows requests so naive rate-limit WAFs don't see a sustained burst.

recipes.title: Time-based only against a quiet endpoint
recipes.details: Forces blind time-based detection — useful when nothing else gives feedback. --time-sec sets the SLEEP() duration; raise on noisy networks.

tips: Always start from a Burp request file (`-r req.txt`). It's the only reliable way to carry session cookies, CSRF tokens, and exact headers.
Use `--batch` everywhere — interactive prompts ruin scripted runs and don't add information you couldn't supply via flags.
Crank --level / --risk slowly. The defaults catch most simple injections; bumping to 3 / 2 catches a lot more but multiplies request count.
When you suspect injection but sqlmap finds nothing, route through Burp with --proxy and watch what it actually sends — the parameter might not be where you think.
--technique restricts payloads. If the app is responsive but sqlmap finds nothing on default, try --technique=T with a longer --time-sec.
Output lands in `~/.local/share/sqlmap/output/<target>/`. CSV dumps, log file, session DB — back this up per engagement.
Tamper scripts compose left-to-right. Test them one at a time first; combinations can mangle payloads in unexpected ways.
limitations: Only finds the injections it has payloads for — second-order injection (data stored now, executed later in another query) needs --second-url, and even then is fragile.
Modern WAFs (Cloudflare, AWS WAF, F5 ASM) trip on sqlmap traffic almost immediately. Tamper scripts help against simple ones; not against modern ML-backed WAFs.
--os-shell / --os-pwn need very specific preconditions (DBA role, writable webroot, supported stack). Most modern hardened deployments break at least one.
Long --dump-all runs against large databases produce gigabytes of CSV. Always scope with -D / -T / -C first.
Testing applications you do not have written authorisation to test is illegal in most jurisdictions.
